entry.title: The Harry Maguire Character
entry.content:
Everyone remembers the Harry Maguire Banter Era. If you are a United fan you might have even taken part in it. I hate to admit I did. The memes and youtube shorts flooded with Harry’s misteps after every United game. All the pundits, the fans were just hammering Harry after every game. United’s instagram post comments sections unanimously echoed “Maguire Out” as the solution to all of United’s problems. And then of course Maguire got benched to the joy of a lot of United fans. The downfall from United captain to United bench was complete. It looked like it was down and out for Harry. It seemed like there was no way back. Alas! I was so wrong.

United signed Harry Maguire for a price tag in the range of 80 million euros. I forget the exact number. The only previous player who I thought deserved that kind of a price tag was Ronaldo. Insane transfer fee. Of course, everyone was super excited to watch Harry play. But obviously Harry turned out to be human. Incredibly good in the air but not the fastest player by any means. Honestly he was kinda slow. And he was clumsy with the ball and he faltered now and then. So yes human when everyone was expecting a ronaldo-esq alien. With that insane price tag, Maguire became the scape goat for all of United’s misfortunes. A laughing stock. (Or a crying over stock?).

So what did Maguire do when he inevitably got benched. He did not look for a wayout. He did not publicly respond to the critism thrown at him all all sides. He stayed put. He dug deep. Stayed patient. Trained really well. And then thanks to injuries he got cameo opportunities in matches and he put on a solid performance. After a series of good cameos, lo and behold, Harry was back in the starting 11. In my opinion this comeback was legendary. Lots of lessons here. Number 1: When things arent going your way, work on yourself, fill the gaps. Number 2: Remember who you are, dont let others decide that for you. Have some character. Number 3: Its much better to let your performance do the talking. Thats the best way to silence the haters. Ladies and Gentleman, thats Harry’s worth.

entry.title: The Solo Hike Chronicles
entry.content:
I was reading a book called No More Mr. Nice Guy. Really good book btw if you ever find yourself just trying to please everyone. Often at the expense of yourself. And the book asked if I have ever spent time by myself. With just my own company without my friends and family. And my answer to that was mostly no except for very brief period of time while driving or in the grocery store. The book suggested me to go on a solo trek. I was always fascinated by the idea of solo treks and that was the final push I needed.

So the following weekend I packed a backpack of essentials to the best of my ability and boarded a taxi, a bus and then a train to go to Kheerganga. My first ever solo trek. This was sometime in December so I did expect a lot of snow or atleast icy trails. So I had packed accordingly. I reached the head of the trek and started hiking. The sights were beautiful. It wasnt actively snowing but it was very cold. Kheerganga is one of its kind because there are natural hot springs on the summit and so I was very excited for them.

I didnt want to stretch the solo trek too long so my plan was to try to do most of the trek on the first day then sleep and then wake up the next day, summit and then go back and board a train/bus back to Delhi sometime around 5pm. On the first day the sun started setting around 5pm. I had a reached a camp site. I was in two mind about calling it a day or to continue on to the next camp site and crashing their instead. I decided to push through the do another camp site so that I am not overloaded the following day. I was just following the beaten path (or so did I thought) so I expected to reach the next camp site in another hour or so well before it became pitch dark. After half an hour or so the number of people walking along with me on the trail had dwindled. I could barely see one or two shadows in the distance. After 15 more minutes I reached a fork in the road. I wasnt sure at all which way to continue going. I decided to take the right and keep going. The route suddenly became very slippery. After a few more steps I slipped and fell and slid about 6 to 8 feet on my fores and stopped on a tree. I realised the tree was hanging over a 30 foot cliff. The tree had just saved my life. After a few deep breath I got back up and got away from the cliff. I realised my knees were bleeding, my back was bleeding my fingers were extremely sore. I need to get to the camp site asap. It was pitch dark now. The iPhone that I was using as flashlight (horrible decision) had died too. I had been hiking for about 2 hours since the previous camp site.

I could see the hill end a few steps ahead so I decided to continue going in the hope that from the top of the hill I would see a few lights and the missing camp site. I reached the top of the hill and looked around and it was just darkness. I could see nothing apart from the few feet around me illuminated by the moon light. I sat down on a rock. My whole life flashed in front of my eyes. I wasnt here if I should spend the night there or if I could survive the cold there the whole night. I honestly wasnt sure if I was gonna make it. I was lost. I was thinking about my parents and my friends and their memory helped conjure some energy. I decided to backtrack. I remembered crossing a river on the way there. I could hear a river so I decided to walk in the direction of that sound. About 30 mins later I did indeed reach the river. My goal was to locate the bridge I had crossed so I needed to walk along the river. I decided to take a right. Fortunately this time that was the right direction and eventually found the bridge. Crossed back and reached the previous camp sight I was at at 5pm around 9 or 10 pm. The camp site was very quiet but I was able to knock on someones tent and rent a space from them for the night.

After a much needed sleep and recovery the next day my body will still aching from all the slips and falls. I was sure I didnt want to keep going by myself. So I waited on the trail for someone to pass. I saw a sherpa going up the trail, I asked him If I could go with him and he agreed. Even though the sherpa was carrying a massive load on his back he was still leaps and bounds faster than me and it was incredibly difficult for me to keep up with him and I came so close to giving up so many times. But I knew if I let him go I wont summit so I stuck with him, I gave that ascent my all and I did indeed summit. The views from the top were a dream and the hot water felt unreal. Since I had a bus to catch I couldnt dilly dally for too long and had to get going back down. The way back was much less troublesome and I made it back for my bus in just the knick of time. The bus might have left about 5 mins after I boarded it.

So what did I get from this crazy adventure. So as it turns out the fork in the road was actually a frozen stream meeting the trail. The stream flows during the day and freezes at night. So I turned right onto a frozen stream and tried hiking it up. I was horribly under prepared for this trek and things almost went very south, Winter treks are dangerous even for a group. But beyond all this. I did achieve my goal of spending time by myself and really digging deep into my will power multiple times. But I realised I did not actually do a solo trek. Many points along the way I needed help. A lot of help. I needed directions. I needed a hot cup of chai. The sherpa who paused for me multiple times. The locals who offered me their tent at 10pm at night. And strangers who didnt know who I was or where I came from, helped me and they were nice to me. I didnt mean anything to them and I hadnt done anything for them. But they went out of their way to put me on my way. I realised just by existing I have value. I have some inherent value by being a human that others seem to see in me. I started seeing that in myself in that trek.

entry.title: Ronaldo Mindset is all you need

link.title: Ronaldo Mindset is all you need

entry.content:
This post means no disrespect to either Havertz or Ronaldo. Both of them are exceptional professional athletes who have earned every right to be where they are.

Cristiano Ronaldo is the one of the most successful athletes in the world. With a trophy cabinet which rivals almost any other, everyone calls him a winner. A champion. On the other hand Kai Havertz is a very talented footballer, but nowhere close in terms of success to Ronaldo. Two footballers. Two very different careers.

My hypothesis is that the thing that separates them so much is not some innate ability or genetic physique. But its their mindset. Ronaldo has an elite mindset. A mindset of a champion. He wants to achieve great things and he wants to help his team win every game. And hes willing to put in the hardwork needed to support his ambitions. Havertz on the other hand doesnt have it. Thats it.

If you have ever seen Ronaldo and Havertz take penalties you would know what I am talking about. When Ronaldo goes up to take a penalty, you instintively know hes going to score. You can see it in his body language, his facial expressions. I bet what going on in his mind is “I want to be the person who scores and helps my team win. What can I do to score? How do I increase my chances? What direction is the keeper going in?”. So his attention is completely focussed on the task which is scoring the penalty. And then of course Ronaldo scores. And when Havertz goes up you already know hes going to miss. His body language is stooped. His face is tense. In his mind hes thinking, “Oh man I dont want to miss this penalty. I dont want to be the person which makes my team lose. What can I do to not miss?”. Hes so focussed on not missing the penalty that he does indeed miss the penalty. In shootouts, Havertz’s penalty record is 3/6 thats 50%. Ronaldo has taken 14 penalties in shootouts converting 12 and missing two with a rate of 84.5%.

I have tried adopting Ronaldo’s “I want to win” mindset instead of Havertz’s “I dont want to lose” mindset in tense situations very effectively. When I am about to give an important presentation or if I am playing a tennis match and on a decisive point, focussing on winning increases your odds of success dramatically. Try it! You wont regret it! Although I single out Havertz in this post, there are tons of footballers who are less successful than Ronaldo. Havertz is just an example to drive a point home. So yeah Ronaldo mindset is all you need.

entry.title: Leaning into your Natural Advantage
entry.content:
There is conventional wisdom about what one should do, where should one live and how should they do it which is generally applicable to everyone. And then there are natural advantages which are uniquely applicable to you. Its quite tempting to not do the work and ignore your natural advantages and follow the herd because its easy but it will always lead to a net suboptimal outcome for you.

So why is leaning into your natural advantage so important? While doing anything new you usually start at level -2 because of lack of experience, unknown unknowns, starting inertia, etc.. A natural advantage allows you to start at level >=0 which dramatically increases your odds of success. The bigger the natural advantage the higher the odds of success.

If you are convinced, the first step is to identify your natural advantage. Sometimes its pretty obvious, sometimes its a little more subtle. Your natural advantage can be anything like there is a macro trend that you find yourself in the middle of, you have a family business that aligns with your interests, you are uniquely experienced in a certain industry or a profession, you have certain important industry connections related to a business opportunity. The best natural advantage is you are extremely interested in something or you get a lot of joy out of it. So it helps knowing yourself well and the best way to know yourself is trying out a lot of things and observing yourself doing those things. I love robotics and engineering so I consider that as my strong natural advantage. Everyone usually has multiple natural advantages.

Once you find your natural advantages you should tap into them. Dont leave your natural advantage on the table. Lean into it just means dont run away from them, run towards them. Set up your decisions and life so that you are dead centre of the intersection of all your natural advantages. This can be hard. This sometimes involves not listening to the conventional wisdom. Going against the wind currents. A lot of your peers might even find what you are doing surprising or borderline stupid because only you can see your natural advantages as clearly as they are. But believe in the magic of the natural advantage and make sure you lean into them to maximise your potential!
entry.summary: Identifying your natural advantages and exploiting them

entry.title: Practicing vulnerability
entry.content:
Life principles!

One of my main takeaways from 2025 is practicing vulnerability. Being vulnerable means to put yourself out there even if the outcome is uncertain.

From an early age I have often avoided rejection like the plague. Not doing something which has a chance of failure. And also taking failures very personally like it reflects on my entire being. I played a lot of competitive sports growing up but I also quit most of them whenever I faced an intense failure. Because I wanted to avoid the feeling of failure. Because I wanted to avoid being a failure.

Faking perfection is also something I have found myself doing. Trying to hide my faults. Posing to be someone I am not. Trying to look cool. Get attention. All this to ensure certain results. Or match a certain perfect image of myself in my head. Although this strategy never worked. So because of all this being vulnerable was really hard. Saying something that I want to say. Doing something that I want to do, whenever there is a risk of failure. So I always avoided it.

I discovered the growth mindset and getting better at something through repetition later in life. I realised that just because I am bad at something does not mean I will be bad at it forever and I can work on it, I can improve at it. There are also some things that are less important to me that I wont be good at and thats okay.

So over the past few years, with these new found realisations I have been on a journey to be more and more vulnerable. I have challenged my inner beliefs about myself and the world (fluid identity). I ask for feedback from friends and family that I trust (growth mindset). And I practice vulnerability by allowing myself in situations where the outcome is uncertain and I just be myself. Sometimes theres success and sometimes theres failure and thats okay but life goes on either way. By being vulnerable I open up a chance of success which is otherwise none. Also wheres the fun in life if you always know how your life will turn out!
entry.summary: Risk free life is reward free life

entry.title: Spirit of Silicon Valley
entry.content:
Since I am leaving silicon valley, I write this so that I can always remember the best of the valley and take it with me whereever I go. In my mind, the spirit of silicon valley is about unbridled optimism, huge Ambition to change the world/ invent the future, Hustle culture and great talent.

Unbridled optimism is central to the ethos of the valley. The belief that future is going to be great and advancements in science and technology is the way forward. Of course any technology is a double edged sword but believing that the positives far outweigh the negatives.

Big ambitions. Most if not all, the start ups in the valley are set out to change the world. Of course not all do but that is the minimum goal of the start up. And how do they change the world? By inventing the future. By making big bets on the future or improving the present in some substantial way. Sam Altman famously said it is easier to do hard things and it is to do easy things. People in the valley have big ambitions and you are much more likely to get their support (engineers/ investors) if you are working on something equally ambitiuous.

Inventing the future is hard but people in the valley want to do it really fast on a 5 to 10 year time scale. This leads to a crazy hustle culture of engineers immersed in doing something super important to them and the world. 12 hour days, weekends, I have definitely seen it all (or not?). But the energy is infectious as though you are gonna miss out on all the fun if you dont do it too.

The above three features of the valley attracts the best engineers, builders, designers and creators from all over the world. Who are also optimistic about the future. Who all want to be part of building the future. Who are also ambitious and hard working and ready to give it all. And who also share the same sense of urgency. And this leads to a pipeline of talent which leads to the valley. So engineers and entrepreneurs here get to work with the best people and that is super rewarding. Working with a team who is at the top of their fields is insanely cool and this is an experience I will cherish forever!
entry.summary: Lessons and values from the valley

entry.title: Yes, AND?
entry.content:
The cool wall painting I used to see everyday on my commute home from work!

Improv is an art of public speaking where performers improvise the characters, the scene and the premise with a goal to entertain the audience. My original motive to study and practice improv was to only get better at thinking on my feet but I found myself applying improv to my daily life with great results.

One of the main rules of improv is that every scene builds up incrementally and when things are said they become facts, you cannot negate them. This is the famous “Yes, AND?” rule in improv. Yes AND is so great because it makes you a much better listener, to add to something you need to first listen to whats being said. Furthermore my usual reaction in real life to new ideas is “But this wont work because”. But this is a really bad reaction because every idea initially is a bad idea and you need to give it some space for it to nurture. Ever since improv, I always apply “Yes AND” to new ideas and that has made me a much better collaborator and team player.

Initially, after improv scenes I used to be quite quick to judge a scene as a “bad scene” or a “good scene”. But one of the things you learn during improv is there is no such thing as a good scene or a bad scene, they are all just Improv scenes! It’s a strangely liberating realization. Just like there is no day as a good day or a bad day. Its just a day! Some days are more productive than others, some days are super stressful but they are all getting you somewhere and the productive days wouldnt exist without the stressful days. So you end up accepting all the days just the same. Now the day to day variability doesnt affect me and helps me put in my best regardless of the result.

In improv, usually the most entertaining scenes are where the performers make strong choices about their characters and then really commit to those choices. If its a peas in a pod scene and you are being a monkey be the most monkey you can be. If its a weird person normal person scene with a teacher who loves talking about his pet cat during the math class, be the weirdest cat loving teacher you can be. The most successful human lives are similar. They care deeply about a few things and then they really devote themselves to it. The Gandhis, the Martin Luther Kings, they made strong choices about what they care about and boy they committed to those choices. So yeah make strong choices, really get the hell behind those choices and dont look back. Be yourself, be that person, rule the world!

entry.title: Thoughts on feelings on superiority and unchecked concentration of power

link.title: Thoughts on feelings on superiority and unchecked concentration of power

entry.content:
I visited the Resistance Museum and Anne Frank’s House in Amsterdam this week. The Resistance Museum is a peek into life in the Netherlands under Nazi Germany.

During the war all the European powers heavily exploited their colonies for mineral resources, natural gas and food supplies. One such colony was Indonesia which was ruled by the Netherlands. This colony had a tiered racist structure with the native Indonesian at the bottom, then the Dutch-Indonesian mixed race and the Dutch people at the top. The native Indonesian people were used for all kinds of slave labour.

Then many of these European powers including the Netherlands themselves were ruled by Nazi Germany and got a taste of what is like to be ruled by an unjust foreign power. Nazi Germany carried out a forced extradition and murder of millions of jewish citizens to concentration camps. The Indonesian colony of the Netherlands was attacked and taken over by Japan. Although initially Japan promised to free the Indonesians from the Dutch rule, they turned to be just as bad as the Dutch and continued their oppression and also oppressed their Dutch rulers into forced labour.

To me the recurring theme is that unchecked power in the hands of anyone has always been abused time and time again. Humans are selfish and they have used that power to serve selfish interests even at the cost of some others. Concentration of power if very harmful and we should always aspire towards a world where the power is kept checked by its own people and has counter balances in case the check fails.

Nazi Germany was also famous for having feelings of superiority over other races. These were also similar to the feelings of supriority that the white-skinned European colonisers had over their colored skin colonies. Japan during the WW2 also had similar feelings about their own race.

Humans have often mistakenly attributed success and progress as a sole result of their own genes. When the reality is much more nuanced than that. Success/ progress is almost always a combination of hardwork, some random privilege, lucky opportunity and other complex factors which worked out in your favour. But human psychology is known to make simplifying assumptions especially when making conclusions about third party experiences (trying to explain your neighbours success). The simplifying assumption about the “feelings of superiority” in this case is a very costly one because it led to multiple generations of oppression of some people by others and the famous world wars.

All the above is also valid for “feelings of inferiority”. More often than not a feeling of inferiority is the result of failures, self or peer brain washing and a lack of growth mindset. The failures themselves have root causes which are also extremely nuanced and are often a lot of environmental factors which are beyond anyone’s control.

Feelings of superiority and inferiority do much more harm than any good. It leads to an unfair society with differential treatment which has no basis in reality. A rule, society or any institution rooted in such feelings is never sustainable and as history has indicated it usually implodes sooner or later. A world we should aspire to build is the opposite of this, which celebrates everyones individuality, distributes opportunities based on merit and correctly attribute cause of success backed by rational reasoning.

A good follow up exercise to this reading is: what feelings of superiority or inferiority do you harbor? Are they rooted in reality or just simplying assumptions? Are you comfortable challenging these feelings if they are no longer serving you or those around you?
entry.summary: Some scattered thoughts after a visit to the Resistance Museum and Anne Frank's House in Amsterdam

entry.title: US Moveout Checklist
entry.content:
I started working on this checklist about a month before departure and I mostly did this along with my job, so mornings, evenings and weekends.

Finances

  1. Clear splitwise
  2. Close all subscriptions
    • Gym membership
    • Spotify
  3. Check all bank deposits
    • How do I maintain status in Chase?
      • Need 1500$ in checking account at all times
      • Decided to close the savings account
      • Need 1-2 transactions from checking account every year
    • Do I need the PNC account or should I close it?
      • Going to close the PNC account
  4. Check annual bonus
  5. Finish taxes
  6. Atleast 3 months of bank statements/ Pay stubs
  7. How to preserve credit in the US?
    • Downgrade credit card? Why pay 95$ a year?
  8. 401k?
    • Both Nimble and Woven, I am gonna just keep it here to grow
  9. Health Saving Account
    • Transfer funds out
    • Close the account
    • https://my.healthequity.com/ClientLogin.aspx
  10. Will my finance apps work in India?
  11. Nimble stocks
    • Make sure you are able to login to Carta

Miscellaneous

  1. Buy New Laptop
  2. Buy phone for gargi
  3. Sell current laptop
  4. Buy Apple Watch for Dad?

Visa

  1. Inform CMU (24 month final evaluation)
  2. Inform homeland security
    • CMU should do this

Phone Number

  1. Port to international?
    • Find solution, talk to Hardik
    • Just gonna keep tmobile for now

House

  1. What to do about electricity, trash and stuff like that
  2. Sell everything
  3. Make a list of items to sell (Talk to Bruce)
  4. Get large heavy duty boxes from Home Depot and start packing
    • Ship things to India
  5. What to do about your mail? Change address on everything?
    • USPS
    • Drivers license
    • Bank accounts
  6. Security Deposit?
    • Schedule meeting with Landlord
    • Check how much you paid
  7. Hand over plant watering duties
  8. Port xfinity membership
  9. Mattress removal
    • https://goloadup.com/mountain-view/mattress-removal/?srsltid=AfmBOoptxwgzWzVFK-F1xcgIEu3tZp4ifneVpd8uuiuPmZza7D7Svdu8

Car

  1. Ask what it takes to return car and stuff
    • Selling car to vaze
  2. Schedule car servicing
  3. Close the fastrak account

Work

  1. Decide on last day: 3rd May
  2. Communicate with people that you are leaving
    • Romain/ Viktor/ Jake/ Marvin
    • Hang with coworkers?

Travel Plan

  1. Flight tickets to India
  2. European visa appointment
  3. Back up plan if visa gets rejected
  4. Finalise itinerary make new bookings
entry.summary: Checklist that I followed while moving from US to India

entry.title: Decision Making
entry.content:
In the Wuthering Heights movie, Cathy is faced with a dilemma that is not quite uncommon. Follow her gut and marry heathcliff and face a probable life of poverty or follow her brain and marry into a much richer family. As it turns out she follows her brain and does not like the decision at all.

The most successful decisions lie somewhere in between where you follow your gut but not blindly. If your gut says that you want to be a model but you are very short and the odds are stacked against you and you are probably not going to make it. Most gut based decisions need some kind of reality check using your rational mind or “brain”. However these gut based decisions or “intuitions” are very important. They hint at you what outcomes will give you the utmost joy. So its important to be able to record them or listen to them and them guide or project them onto the manifold of reality which is usually made up of laws of physics, your skills, talents, background and experiences. So its about guiding what you want based on how well you know yourself and the world. Its about reconciling your wants with what’s real. However this does not mean that hard things are not realistic. If hard things are achievable and worth doing then they are still worth pursuing.

All children have is intuition because they dont have a well developed reasoning based brain yet. They also dont have many experiences so their model of reality is very limited. So children’s responses to “what they want to become when they get older” are based on the limited data they have and their intuitions and no reasoning. Getting older is the process of aligning your intuitions with reality through your improved model of the world and better brain reasoning. On the other hand children are really good at listening to their intuitions which is the ability adults seem to lack when they get older.

Perhaps this is also in line with Daniel Kahneman’s System 1 vs System 2 thinking where he presents all kinds of biases that the human brain is plagued with and strongly endorses System 2 thinking to overcome these biases and make good long term decisions.

In the movie, Cathy does realise her unsatisfactory outcome but due to a series of events (mainly because of the Nelly character) is never able to correct it. This is mainly a movie gimmick where all the characters acted incredibly stupid. In real life however despite your best efforts if you find yourself in a a very unsatisfactory outcome, you are able to backtrack and correct your decision most of the time. Albeit at the cost of some money, time and energy.

In summary, listen to your intuitions, align them with the reality with good System 2 reasoning and then give it your best shot, if it still doesnt work out you can always try and backtrack and correct your decision.
